name: Bun
summary: Zig-implemented all-in-one JavaScript runtime, bundler, package manager, and test runner built on JavaScriptCore (Apple's Safari engine) rather than V8; trades V8 ecosystem for cold-start latency and unified toolchain.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Jarred Sumner / Oven's **Bun** (since 2022; v1.0 September 2023) is the production Zig data point in the JavaScript runtime space — an all-in-one runtime, bundler, package manager, and test runner implemented in approximately 700 KLOC of Zig over the **JavaScriptCore** engine (Apple's Safari engine), explicitly *not* V8. The architectural choice: pick the engine optimised for cold-start latency (JSC's interpreter-tier startup beats V8 Ignition's by a wide margin), pair it with Zig's manual memory management and zero-cost FFI, and unify what Node ships as separate tools (`npm`, `webpack`, `jest`, `tsc --watch`) into one binary with shared runtime infrastructure.

sections.title: I/O loop and Zig implementation
sections.content: Distinct from Node.js (V8 + libuv): Bun's I/O loop is custom-written in Zig, supports `io_uring` on Linux, and exposes Node-compatible APIs (`fs`, `http`, `child_process`, `cluster`) plus Web-standard APIs (`fetch`, `WebSocket`, `Response`) on the same runtime. The package manager is content-addressed (a global cache shared across projects), much faster than `npm install` on cold cache, and uses a binary lockfile format (`bun.lock`). The bundler is Webpack-API-compatible but written in Zig and competitive with esbuild and Rolldown.

sections.title: Trade-offs vs Node and Deno
sections.content:
Status (as of 2026-04): production-stable; deployed at scale by Vercel (some Edge functions), Railway (default Node-replacement), several SaaS startups. Distinct from **Deno** (V8-based and capability-sandboxed): Bun targets the "drop-in faster Node" deployment where capability sandboxing is not the goal. Distinct from **Node.js**: JavaScriptCore-specific behaviour (different from V8's) sometimes surfaces as compatibility gaps for code that relied on V8 internals.

The lesson generalises: a unified-toolchain runtime in a systems language with manual memory management can leapfrog the Node-as-separate-tools baseline, with the catch that engine-specific behaviour sometimes surfaces as compatibility gaps. Bun is also the largest production Zig codebase to-date — an existence proof that Zig's ergonomics scale to hundreds of KLOC without imposing the C++-template debt that motivated V8's complexity.
